title: Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2

abridged.content:
Mixed venous saturation - the global oxygen balance signal

SvO₂ is oxygen saturation in pulmonary artery blood after venous blood from the whole body has mixed. It reflects the balance between oxygen delivery and oxygen consumption. A usual range is 60-80%, with many CTICU targets around ≥65-70% when the rest of the picture agrees.

ScvO₂ is central venous oxygen saturation from the SVC/right atrial region. It is not identical to SvO₂ because it does not fully include lower body and coronary sinus contributions.

Low SvO₂ usually means high extraction from low delivery, high demand, or both. High SvO₂ is not automatically good; it may indicate impaired extraction, shunting, sepsis, hypothermia, deep sedation, mitochondrial dysfunction, or sampling error.

expanded.content:
Mixed venous saturation - oxygen left after tissue extraction

SvO₂ is the percentage of haemoglobin still saturated with oxygen after blood has circulated through the body and returned to the pulmonary artery. It is true mixed venous blood because it includes venous return from the SVC, IVC, and coronary sinus.

SvO₂ is a global balance marker. It depends on cardiac output, haemoglobin, SaO₂, and VO₂. It does not identify which organ is ischaemic, but it tells you whether whole-body oxygen extraction is under strain.

Normal range

Usual adult SvO₂ is about 60-80%. Many CTICU clinicians use ≥65-70% as a practical target if lactate, urine output, CI, and perfusion agree. Trends matter. A fall from 75% to 58% is important even if the patient has not yet crashed.

Low SvO₂

Low SvO₂ means tissues have extracted more oxygen. Causes include low cardiac output, anaemia, hypoxaemia, fever, shivering, agitation, pain, seizures, sepsis, and increased work of breathing.

CauseMechanismCluesFocus
Low COLess O₂ delivered/minLow CI, cool, oliguria, lactateFix preload/rhythm/contractility/afterload/obstruction
AnaemiaLow CaO₂Low Hb, bleedingAssess bleeding/transfusion need
HypoxaemiaLow SaO₂Low SpO₂/PaO₂Improve oxygenation
Shivering/feverHigh VO₂Temperature, shiveringReduce demand

High SvO₂

High SvO₂ may mean delivery is high and demand is low, but it can also be pathological. In sepsis, microcirculatory shunting, mitochondrial dysfunction, severe inflammation, hypothermia, deep sedation, or sampling error, tissues may fail to extract oxygen, so venous saturation remains high despite tissue hypoxia. High SvO₂ with rising lactate is therefore concerning.

SvO₂ versus ScvO₂

ScvO₂ is measured from central venous blood, usually near the SVC/right atrium. It is easier to obtain but not the same as SvO₂. It does not fully represent lower body or coronary sinus blood. In shock, the relationship becomes unpredictable, so trends and context are essential.

Measurement issues

Samples can be wrong if drawn from the wrong port, contaminated by infusions, taken during catheter wedging, or affected by catheter migration/calibration issues. If the number does not match the patient, verify source, waveform, port, sampling technique, calibration, Hb, SaO₂, temperature, rhythm, and recent therapy changes.

Bedside use

  1. Confirm technical validity.

  2. Check delivery variables: CO/CI, Hb, SaO₂, MAP.

  3. Check demand variables: fever, shivering, pain, work of breathing, sepsis.

  4. Compare with lactate, urine output, skin perfusion, and acid-base status.

  5. Act on the pattern, not the number alone.

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - Mixed Venous Saturations

Pearl 1 - SvO₂ is oxygen left over

It falls when tissues extract more because delivery is low or demand is high.

Pearl 2 - High SvO₂ is not always reassuring

High SvO₂ with rising lactate suggests impaired extraction, shunt, sepsis, or error.

Pearl 3 - ScvO₂ is not SvO₂

ScvO₂ trends are useful but cannot be assumed identical to pulmonary artery mixed venous saturation.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - Mixed Venous Saturations

PAC measures true SvO₂ from pulmonary artery blood. Confirm PA waveform and avoid wedged sampling.

CVC can provide ScvO₂, but samples must avoid infusion contamination.

Co-oximetry measures saturation and can detect dyshemoglobins if available.

Haemodynamic monitor should display SvO₂ alongside CI, MAP, CVP, PAWP, SVR/PVR, temperature, and vasoactive support.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - Mixed Venous Saturations

SvO₂ is usually 60-80%. A practical target is often ≥65-70% in CTICU if lactate, urine output, CI, and perfusion agree. ScvO₂ is often >70%, but it is not identical. Low values require assessment for low CO, anaemia, hypoxaemia, or high VO₂. High values with deterioration require concern for impaired extraction, shunting, sepsis, hypothermia, sedation, or error.

quizQuestions.question: What is the central bedside concept in Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2?
options: Interpret the value as part of a haemodynamic pattern, not as an isolated number
Treat every abnormal number with fluid
Ignore the waveform and rely only on MAP
Use SpO₂ as the only marker of perfusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2 must be interpreted in context. CTICU physiology depends on patterns across pressure, flow, filling, oxygen delivery, demand, waveforms, and clinical response.

quizQuestions.question: A post-cardiac surgery patient deteriorates and the Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2 data conflict with the clinical picture. What is the safest action?
options: Validate the measurement and integrate it with rhythm, waveform, lactate, urine output, echo, and perfusion
Assume the single number is correct and treat it alone
Ignore the patient because the monitor is always right
quizQuestions.explanation: The safest CTICU approach is to validate the measurement, check equipment and waveform quality, then interpret it against the whole clinical pattern before acting.

quizQuestions.question: Why is isolated interpretation dangerous in Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2?
options: Because preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, ventilation, oxygen content, and metabolic demand interact
Because cardiac physiology has no relationship to clinical signs
Because all post-operative patients have identical targets
Because monitors cannot display useful information
quizQuestions.explanation: Isolated interpretation is dangerous because CTICU haemodynamics are interactive. The same value may mean different things depending on ventricular function, vascular tone, rhythm, ventilation, oxygen delivery, and surgical context.
